summary:
The Statue of Liberty, dedicated on 28 October 1886 on Bedloe's Island (now Liberty Island) in New York Harbor, was a gift from France to the United States commemorating the friendship of the two republics and the shared ideals of liberty that had inspired both nations' revolutions. Designed by the French sculptor Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi with an interior iron structure engineered by Gustave Eiffel (who would soon build the [[event:3772|Eiffel Tower]]), it became one of the world's most recognized symbols.

The statue grew from an idea proposed by the French political thinker Édouard de Laboulaye at a dinner party in 1865, celebrating the recent American abolition of slavery and the endurance of the republic through the [[event:2536|Civil War]]. The project was conceived as a joint venture: France would provide the statue itself, the United States would build and pay for the pedestal. The American fundraising was slow and often difficult, requiring a campaign by Joseph Pulitzer's New York World that raised funds from ordinary citizens through small donations.

The statue's copper figure — 46 meters tall, standing on an 89-meter pedestal — depicts a robed woman holding a torch aloft in her right hand and a tablet inscribed '4 July 1776' in her left. Her crown bears seven rays representing the seven continents. Emma Lazarus's sonnet 'The New Colossus' (1883), mounted on the pedestal in 1903, reinterpreted the statue as a beacon to immigrants with the famous lines welcoming 'the huddled masses yearning to breathe free.' This immigrant welcome, rather than the republican idealism of the original concept, became the statue's dominant meaning in American culture, reflecting the mass immigration transforming the United States in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.
